title: Im Leslie Knope
weakest_section: 13:20-14:00 range with Tom's promotional material gags

jokes.text: Absotootly, I am. Very sorry that I just used that word.
jokes.setup: Leslie is asked about running for office and needs to sound professional
jokes.punchline: She creates a ridiculous word mashup and immediately apologizes
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie character moment - her enthusiasm creates awkward neologisms, then her self-awareness kicks in. The immediate self-correction makes it endearing rather than just cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm ready to serve the people of Pawnee with integrity and professionalism.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Together, we can build a more... prosperous... and munificent future!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, did I just say munificent? I meant magnificent. Or... munificent is a word, right? Either way, I'm sorry.

jokes.text: Leslie's confident 'Nope' after being asked about scandals
jokes.setup: Serious question about potential scandals in her past
jokes.punchline: Completely confident denial right before we learn about her secret relationship
jokes.explanation: Classic dramatic irony - Leslie's confidence is immediately undercut by the next scene revealing her secret. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I have nothing to hide. My entire life is an open book. There are no secrets, no scandals, nothing embarrassing in my past whatsoever.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, we just found photos of you and—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, well, there's that one thing.

jokes.text: Multiple 'Yay!' exchanges followed by 'Wait. What does that mean about you and Ben? I don't know. I think it's going to be really bad.'
jokes.setup: Enthusiastic celebration about Leslie running for office
jokes.punchline: Sudden realization that it creates a major problem with her secret relationship
jokes.explanation: Nice emotional whiplash from joy to dread. The repeated 'yay' makes the crash more effective.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is it! This is my moment! I'm running for City Council and I'm going to win!

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Leslie, that's amazing! We're so excited for you!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know, right? I've been preparing for this my whole—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben. I can't be with Ben. The ethics violations. He's the Deputy Director.

jokes.text: Uh-oh! Do you want to go back to saying 'yay'? Yes, please. Yay! Yay!
jokes.setup: Leslie realizes the implications of running for office
jokes.punchline: Ann offers to return to blissful ignorance, which Leslie immediately accepts
jokes.explanation: Smart callback that shows both characters' personalities - Ann's supportiveness and Leslie's occasional need to avoid reality.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You know what? I could go back to not knowing any of this stuff. Ignorance is bliss.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes. Please do that.

jokes.text: No, my other ex-wife, Tammy. Tammy One.
jokes.setup: Someone mentions seeing Ron's ex-wife Tammy
jokes.punchline: Reveals he has multiple ex-wives named Tammy, and this is the worse one
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson absurdity. The numbering system for multiple wives with the same name is both bizarre and completely believable for his character.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have been married twice. Both times to a woman named Tammy.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tammy Two was the worst thing that ever happened to me.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: But Tammy One... Tammy One tried to sabotage the entire Parks Department. So it's a tough call.

jokes.text: I have accrued 228 personal days. Starting right now, I'm using all of them.
jokes.setup: Ron needs to avoid his ex-wife
jokes.punchline: His solution is to use an impossibly large number of saved vacation days
jokes.explanation: The specific number '228' makes it funnier than just 'a lot.' Shows Ron's methodical nature even in panic mode.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have accumulated 3,400 hours of unused vacation days. I'm taking them all at once. My ex-wife Tammy will never find me.

jokes.text: Also, I keep a sizable supply of ground chuck in my desk. Remove it or it will begin to smell.
jokes.setup: Ron is giving Leslie instructions before his extended absence
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions keeping raw meat in his office furniture
jokes.explanation: Perfectly random Ron detail. The matter-of-fact delivery of something completely inappropriate for an office environment.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm keeping some raw meat in my desk drawer. It's for emergencies.

jokes.text: Hey, Ron. Move. Oh, jeez.
jokes.setup: Ron tries to escape through the office
jokes.punchline: Tammy One's commanding presence and Leslie's horrified reaction to seeing her
jokes.explanation: Sets up Tammy One as intimidating. Leslie's reaction sells the horror.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, wait! Where are you going?

dialogue.speaker: Tammy One
dialogue.line: Ronald.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, no.

jokes.text: Warning. High levels of swagger coming through.
jokes.setup: Tom returns to the office
jokes.punchline: Announces himself with maximum self-importance
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom overconfidence. The word 'swagger' and treating it like a hazardous material warning is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hello, everybody. It is I, Tom Haverford, entrepreneur, visionary, and small business owner. I have returned.

jokes.text: What exactly do we do? Let's just say, it's too hard to explain.
jokes.setup: Tom describing his new company
jokes.punchline: Admits he can't actually explain what his business does
jokes.explanation: Perfect satire of startup culture and Tom's tendency to prioritize style over substance. The 'too hard to explain' is both pretentious and an admission of emptiness.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I can't actually explain what my business does.

jokes.text: Donna, you look amazing. How are the kids? I don't have kids. Wow. How long has it been? Three weeks.
jokes.setup: Tom acting like he's been gone forever
jokes.punchline: He's forgotten basic facts about his coworker after only three weeks
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - starts with normal small talk, reveals he doesn't know her at all, then the punchline that it's only been three weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh man, it feels like I've been gone for years! So much has changed!

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Tom, you were only gone three weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, who are you again?

jokes.text: How do you feel about my stance on Egyptian debt relief? It probably won't come up in a local city council election.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking about her position papers
jokes.punchline: She's over-prepared for local politics with international issues
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie - her thoroughness becomes absurd when applied to local politics. The advisor's deadpan response sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So what do you think of my position papers?

dialogue.speaker: Political advisor
dialogue.line: Leslie, these are great, but you're running for City Council in Pawnee, Indiana. You have forty pages on Middle East peace accords.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I like to be thorough.

dialogue.speaker: Political advisor
dialogue.line: You also wrote a comprehensive trade policy with the European Union. Pawnee doesn't have trade relations with the European Union.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Not yet.

jokes.text: While other girls were playing with Barbies, I was playing with a Geraldine Ferraro action figure, that I made myself from a picture of her that I glued onto a Popsicle stick.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining her lifelong political ambitions
jokes.punchline: Reveals she made a DIY action figure of a female politician as a child
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character detail that's both funny and touching. The specificity of the popsicle stick construction and choice of Geraldine Ferraro shows Leslie's unique childhood obsessions.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: When I was a little girl, I made an action figure out of a Barbie doll and some pipe cleaners. I named her Senator Barbara and she fought crime and passed legislation.

jokes.text: Ladies. Just ladies. Meeting for the ladies' yacht club. Anchors away, ladies.
jokes.setup: Ben asks who Leslie was meeting with
jokes.punchline: Terrible improvised cover story that makes no sense
jokes.explanation: Leslie's panic creates increasingly ridiculous explanations. The 'anchors away' is desperate overselling.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Who were you meeting with?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, just my... my dentist. Dr. Teeth McFloss. He's very exclusive. Only takes patients on Tuesdays and Saturdays, but also Wednesdays. He's very flexible with his schedule. We were discussing my gums, which are — I'm very proud of them.

jokes.text: Oh, Ann. You beautiful, naive, sophisticated newborn baby.
jokes.setup: Ann suggests Leslie could keep sneaking around
jokes.punchline: Leslie's response is a string of contradictory adjectives
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie word salad. The contradiction between 'sophisticated' and 'newborn baby' plus the layering of adjectives is pure character comedy gold.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I can't sneak around, Ann. I'm not sneaky. I'm loud and aggressive and I have a very distinctive walk.

jokes.text: A penis. What? Oh, my God. Oh. It's emailing me, too.
jokes.setup: Leslie checking her emails
jokes.punchline: Treating the photo like it's actively sending emails
jokes.explanation: The personification of the photo as an active agent is unexpectedly funny. Simple but effective misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ann, look at this email. It has a photo of us from the gala attached.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Stop emailing me pictures of yourself! I already have enough photos of you!

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Leslie, I didn't send that.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, your photo did. Look at it. Stop it. No more emails!

jokes.text: You know what? Respect. That's a baller move. Pun intended.
jokes.setup: Learning about the inappropriate photo
jokes.punchline: Andy admires the audacity and acknowledges his accidental pun
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy response - completely inappropriate admiration plus self-aware pun recognition shows his childlike perspective.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wow, that's... that's pretty bold. I mean, you gotta admire the audacity.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, did I just make a pun? That was accidental.

jokes.text: If I could go back in time and cut your eyeballs out, I would. Wow, that is so sweet. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Chris threatens Jerry for the inappropriate photo
jokes.punchline: Jerry completely misinterprets the violent threat as kindness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry misunderstanding. The contrast between Chris's graphic threat and Jerry's grateful response is absurd and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Jerry, if you ever do that again, I'm going to break every bone in your body.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, Chris, that's the nicest thing anyone's ever said to me.

jokes.text: Oh! She can do Perd Hapley. Seriously. Can you do his TV show?
jokes.setup: Asking Leslie to do a TV interview
jokes.punchline: The advisors are excited about Leslie's ability to imitate the interviewer
jokes.explanation: Sets up the anticipation for Leslie's Perd impression. Meta humor about the show's recurring character.

dialogue.speaker: Political Advisor 1
dialogue.line: Leslie, we'd like you to do a TV interview.

dialogue.speaker: Political Advisor 2
dialogue.line: We've heard you're great at imitating interviewers.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Well, I do have a talent for that.

dialogue.speaker: Political Advisor 1
dialogue.line: That's perfect! You can anticipate their questions and prepare better answers.

dialogue.speaker: Political Advisor 2
dialogue.line: This is going to be amazing!

jokes.text: Black print, black background. It's the coolest possible color scheme. It's also a strong magnet, so keep it out of your wallet. It will destroy your credit cards. Guaranteed.
jokes.setup: Tom giving Andy his business card
jokes.punchline: The 'cool' design is completely illegible and also destructive
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom - prioritizing aesthetics over functionality to an absurd degree. The magnetic property is an unexpected escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: This is my new business card. Pretty cool, right?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Tom, I can't read any of this.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's because it's cool. If you can read it, it's not cool enough.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's also on fire.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's the destructible element. Very exclusive.

jokes.text: Destroy my credit cards. Debt and everything? Mr. Mayor, I promise you that we will find the owner of that wiener, and we will punish him.
jokes.setup: Andy asking about the card's destructive properties
jokes.punchline: His hopeful question about debt relief cuts to Ben's serious phone call about penis investigation
jokes.explanation: Great scene transition comedy. Andy's innocent hope about escaping debt juxtaposed with Ben's professional dignity being destroyed.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Does it have any destructive properties?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: No, it's just a regular card.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So it won't help me with my debt?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: No, Andy, it won't. Now if you'll excuse me, I have to take this call about a penis investigation.

jokes.text: The testicles are like the ears of the genital system. They serve a very important function, but they're not that great to look at.
jokes.setup: Ann mentioning something disturbing about the photo
jokes.punchline: Chris offers an unexpectedly poetic anatomical comparison
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Chris moment - his relentlessly positive personality creates beautiful metaphors for uncomfortable topics. The comparison is both accurate and ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That is disturbing.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Your body is a temple. My body is a finely-tuned instrument of maximum physical potential and performance.

jokes.text: Well, frankly, Perd, it's not that big a deal, if you know what I mean. I don't know what you mean, but it had the cadence of a joke.
jokes.setup: Leslie making a penis size joke on TV
jokes.punchline: Perd's complete failure to understand while recognizing the joke structure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Perd Hapley moment - his complete literal-mindedness while still recognizing comedic patterns. Meta commentary on joke structure itself.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And that's why we need to invest in our parks. Because let's face it, size matters.

dialogue.speaker: Perd
dialogue.line: Well, that's a joke. I recognize the structure of a joke. But I don't understand what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It was a penis joke, Perd.

dialogue.speaker: Perd
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's inappropriate for television.

jokes.text: There you have it, where 'it' is the thing Leslie Knope just said about this situation.
jokes.setup: Perd wrapping up the interview
jokes.punchline: Defines his own catchphrase in the most literal way possible
jokes.explanation: Classic Perd - turning normal TV language into absurdly literal definitions. Shows his complete lack of natural communication skills.

dialogue.speaker: Perd
dialogue.line: And that's the news. Perd Hapley, news anchor, saying the news. You see, I say 'the news' because I am literally saying news to you right now.

jokes.text: It's an L-shaped éclair. The 'L' is for Leslie, for your name.
jokes.setup: Ben trying to be romantic before Leslie breaks up with him
jokes.punchline: Awkward over-explanation of a simple gesture
jokes.explanation: Ben's nervous over-explanation of an obvious gesture. The timing makes it more painful/funny as Leslie is about to break up with him.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, I got you flowers. They're not just flowers—they're a symbol of my deep affection, combined with the traditional romantic gesture that spans centuries of human courtship, representing both the beauty of nature and the fragility of love itself.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, that's... we need to talk.

jokes.text: Well, I started crying because he gave me an eclair. And then we made out and spent the night together. But this morning, in the cold light of day, I cooked breakfast and we made out some more.
jokes.setup: Ann asks how the breakup went
jokes.punchline: Leslie completely failed to break up and did the opposite repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how completely Leslie failed at her mission. The 'cold light of day' setup makes the second failure funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It went great! We're now engaged. We're getting married in the spring. And we're having triplets.

jokes.text: Wow! You opposite of broke up with him. He gave me an éclair, Ann.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining her failure to break up
jokes.punchline: Her defense is just pointing out the obvious gesture that derailed her
jokes.explanation: Leslie's complete inability to resist romantic gestures. The éclair has become her kryptonite, which is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I was going to break up with him, but then he did this thing where he looked at me with those eyes, you know? Those big puppy dog eyes. And I just... I couldn't do it.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Leslie, you can't just not break up with someone because of their eyes.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Have you SEEN his eyes, Ann?

jokes.text: stinkmaster69@alumni.sarahlawrencecollege.edu. Really? Sarah Lawrence? Yeah. I wanted a small college experience.
jokes.setup: Revealing the email address of the photo sender
jokes.punchline: The crude username contrasts with the prestigious liberal arts college
jokes.explanation: Great contrast between the immature email handle and the sophisticated college choice. Joe's earnest explanation makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, who sent this? What's the email address?

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: It's... horndog27@swarthmore.edu

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Swarthmore? That's like, one of the best liberal arts colleges in the country.

jokes.text: There's an old sewage department saying, 'if you've got a nice drain pipe, there's no reason to hide it.' I doubt that's a saying.
jokes.setup: Joe trying to justify his actions
jokes.punchline: Invents obviously fake workplace wisdom using plumbing metaphors
jokes.explanation: Absurd made-up wisdom that sounds plausible for a sewage worker while being obviously inappropriate. Chris's immediate skepticism sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: Look, sometimes you gotta think of the office like a pipe system. You got your main line—that's me—and everything else is just tributaries flowing into my greatness.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: That's not how pipes work.

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: See, that's what I'm talking about. You're clogged up with negativity. You need to flush that out with some positive P-trap energy.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Those are all real plumbing terms and you're using them completely wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: Exactly. That's the kind of can-do attitude that gets results.

jokes.text: And you have the mumps. You might want to get that checked. Sweet. Someone's got mumps on his lumps.
jokes.setup: Revealing the medical diagnosis from the photo
jokes.punchline: Joe's proud wordplay about his own medical condition
jokes.explanation: Joe's complete lack of shame turns a medical emergency into a source of pride. The rhyme makes it even more inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: So what's the diagnosis?

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: Well, the doctor said I have a very rare condition. It's called 'Joe-ing too hard.'

jokes.text: UP high. Anyone? Ron isn't here. His ex-wife, Tammy, came, and he got scared and ran away.
jokes.setup: Joe asking for a high-five celebration
jokes.punchline: Complete silence, then Leslie's matter-of-fact explanation of Ron's cowardice
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Joe's celebration and everyone's disgust, plus casual revelation of Ron's terror. Good awkward beat.

dialogue.speaker: Joe
dialogue.line: High five!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron's afraid of physical contact. He once spent an entire weekend locked in his office because someone sneezed near him.

jokes.text: Well, as acting manager of the Parks Department, I declare that everything you are saying is stupid. Hah! I love you.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to assert authority in Ron's absence
jokes.punchline: April's complete dismissal followed by Andy's random declaration of love
jokes.explanation: Perfect April deadpan dismissal of authority. Andy's random interjection shows his complete disconnection from the conflict.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: As the senior staff member present, I'm taking charge of this office.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No one cares.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I love you, Leslie!

jokes.text: So, take it. But, working at a start-up can be kind of risky. So, don't take it. But Tom told me to follow my dreams. So, take it. But this isn't my dream. So, don't take it.
jokes.setup: Andy asking for career advice
jokes.punchline: Leslie's advice constantly contradicts itself based on each new piece of information
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalating contradiction showing Leslie's overthinking nature. The rapid back-and-forth creates good rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Leslie, I need career advice. Should I stick with my current job or try something new?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, you should absolutely try something new! You need to challenge yourself and grow!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Really? Even though I have a family to support?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, you have a family? No, no, no. You need to stay put. Stability is everything. Family comes first.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But I thought you said I should challenge myself.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but that was before I knew about the family situation. Although, actually, having a family means you should be even MORE ambitious. Show them what's possible! Pursue your dreams!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So which is it?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Both. Neither. Whatever you choose, I will support you 100 percent, but also I will judge you slightly for not picking the other option.

jokes.text: I don't know what to do. Me, neither. You give such good advice. Babe, I love you. You're welcome.
jokes.setup: Leslie's completely unhelpful advice session
jokes.punchline: Andy thinks it was helpful, Leslie accepts credit for terrible advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Andy's gratitude for useless advice and Leslie's willingness to take credit. Shows both their personalities perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So Andy, what's troubling you?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, I've been thinking about quitting my job.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, that's easy. Just don't think about it. Problem solved.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wow, Leslie, that's actually really helpful advice.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know. I'm basically a therapist.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're the worst therapist.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Thank you.

jokes.text: Your inbox is literally filled with penises. Oh, look. Ed Miller from Payroll.
jokes.setup: Ann explaining the consequences of her medical diagnosis
jokes.punchline: Treats the parade of genitals like normal email correspondence
jokes.explanation: The casual recognition of a specific sender amidst the inappropriate content. Ann's professional tone makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Treats the parade of genitals like normal email correspondence

jokes.text: Do you need to get that? No, it's just penises.
jokes.setup: Phone interrupting serious political discussion
jokes.punchline: Leslie dismisses it with maximum casualness
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing. The complete casualness of dismissing penises as routine interruptions is absurd and perfectly delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Hold on, I'm getting a call. Hello? Oh hey! No, no, we're just in the middle of something really important here. Yeah, okay, I'll call you back. Bye!

dialogue.speaker: Political advisor
dialogue.line: Who was that?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: My mom.

jokes.text: It's the entire Sanitation Department, from Muncie. Ugh!
jokes.setup: The penis photo situation continues to escalate
jokes.punchline: Now it's spread to other cities and departments
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how the problem has grown beyond their city. Ann's exasperation sells the absurd scope.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Now it's spread to other cities and departments

jokes.text: I would like a glass of red wine, and I'll take the cheapest one you have because I can't tell the difference.
jokes.setup: Leslie ordering wine while being 'direct and honest'
jokes.punchline: Admits her complete lack of wine sophistication
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's honesty and lack of pretension. Relatable and endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'll have a glass of your cheapest wine. Actually, you know what? I don't know anything about wine. I don't know if I'm supposed to swirl it or sniff it or what. I just know that it makes me feel warm and loose, and I like how it tastes like fruit sometimes.

jokes.text: I'm going to go to the bathroom. The whiz palace, as we like to call it here.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to escape the difficult conversation
jokes.punchline: Uses inappropriate slang for the bathroom at a nice restaurant
jokes.explanation: Leslie's nervous energy creates inappropriate casualness. The phrase 'whiz palace' is both childish and out of place.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I need to use the bathroom. Excuse me, where's the pooper?

jokes.text: If you're going to stay here, there are three rules you need to follow. One, no talk about Tammy One. Two, no talk about Ben. Three, no talk.
jokes.setup: Ron setting ground rules for his hideout
jokes.punchline: The rules escalate to complete silence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron escalation from specific prohibitions to total communication ban. Shows his misanthropy and desire for isolation in crisis.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Welcome to my cabin. Here are the ground rules: no talking during meals, no talking during TV time, and no talking while I'm thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? No talking at all. In fact, I'd prefer it if you didn't even breathe too loudly.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: And if you must exist in this space, do it as quietly as possible. Ideally, stop existing altogether.

jokes.text: You just violated rules number one and three. You lose your coffee privileges.
jokes.setup: Leslie immediately asks about Tammy One
jokes.punchline: Ron enforces his rules with arbitrary punishment
jokes.explanation: Good immediate callback showing Ron's rigid rule enforcement. The coffee punishment is appropriately petty.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So what happened with Tammy One?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: She violated the rules. I enforced them.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What rules? What did she do?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have a list. She breathed too loud during dinner. That's a spanking. She used my toothbrush without asking. That's two spankings. She wore the same outfit twice in one week. That's a spanking and no dessert for a month.

jokes.text: I'd really love to shoot a gun right now. Fishing it is.
jokes.setup: Ron offers hunting, fishing, or drinking
jokes.punchline: Leslie's violent impulse gets redirected to the most peaceful option
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - Ron hears Leslie wants to shoot and immediately steers her toward fishing. Shows his protective instincts.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm offering you three options: hunting, fishing, or drinking.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I choose yoga.

jokes.text: Perfect. Just as a backup, I'm sending you some photos. That will not be necessary. That's me.
jokes.setup: Ben arranging medical screenings to stop the photo sharing
jokes.punchline: Employee already sending photos despite the solution
jokes.explanation: Shows how the problem has gotten completely out of hand. The immediate disregard for the solution is frustratingly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I've arranged for everyone to get comprehensive medical screenings. No more embarrassing photos to share.

dialogue.speaker: Male employee
dialogue.line: That's great, Ben. Thanks for thinking of us.

dialogue.speaker: Male employee
dialogue.line: Hey everyone, check out these X-rays of my spleen!

jokes.text: You think Bethenny Frankel sits behind a desk all day? She makes $100 million a year. How much do you make a year? $101 million.
jokes.setup: Tom using celebrity entrepreneur as inspiration
jokes.punchline: April's impossible claim delivered completely straight
jokes.explanation: Perfect April deadpan lie. The specific number ($101 million) just one-upping Tom makes it funnier. Classic April deflation of pretension.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I've been reading about Richard Branson. That guy's a total inspiration. He started Virgin Records in his parents' basement.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I was born in my parents' basement.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: My parents didn't believe in hospitals. They thought they were a conspiracy by the government to make us dependent on the state. So my mom just squatted over a bucket for six hours while my dad read to her from a pamphlet about natural childbirth that he printed off the internet on our dial-up connection.

jokes.text: That's not what my company does. Although, maybe we'll start.
jokes.setup: Andy saying he doesn't want to work for a logo company
jokes.punchline: Tom admits that's exactly what they might become
jokes.explanation: Shows Tom's complete lack of business focus. He'll pivot to anything that sounds remotely profitable.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I don't want to work for a logo company.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, that's exactly what we might become.

jokes.text: I figure we build a fire, roast the fish we shot, and make s'mores. I don't have the material for s'mores. I do. I always carry emergency s'more rations in my car.
jokes.setup: Leslie planning their wilderness survival
jokes.punchline: Reveals she's prepared for campfire desserts in any situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie over-preparation. The term 'emergency s'more rations' treats marshmallows like survival gear, which is quintessentially Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've prepared for every possible wilderness survival scenario. Hypothermia, bear attacks, flash floods, getting lost for days without food or water.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's... actually impressive.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Thank you! I have freeze-dried meals, a water purification system, emergency flares, a first aid kit, and seventeen different s'mores recipes.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: ...Did you say seventeen s'mores recipes?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: For any campfire dessert emergency. Classic, chocolate chip, peppermint bark, salted caramel—

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You're prepared for dessert emergencies.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You never know when you'll need s'mores.

jokes.text: You know, when I was 12, my brother shot me in the pinky toe with a nail gun. Granted, it was a hilarious prank, and we all had a good laugh.
jokes.setup: Leslie sharing a childhood story
jokes.punchline: Treats a serious injury as normal family fun
jokes.explanation: Dark humor disguised as family bonding. Leslie's positive spin on trauma is both funny and concerning.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: When I was eight, I fell off a horse and broke my arm in two places. My parents took me to the emergency room, and on the way home, we stopped for ice cream. My dad said, 'Well, Leslie, you've learned an important lesson today.' I said, 'What's that, Dad?' He said, 'Never break your arm in just one place.'

jokes.text: You only have nine toes? I have the toes I have. Let's just leave it at that.
jokes.setup: Learning about Leslie's missing toe
jokes.punchline: Ron's mysterious non-answer about his toe situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron deflection that somehow makes his toe situation even more mysterious. His refusal to elaborate is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, why do you only have nine toes?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't want to talk about it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Come on, tell me what happened.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A man is entitled to his mysteries.

jokes.text: Sorry. That was uncalled for.
jokes.setup: Leslie making a joke about Ron's toe count
jokes.punchline: Immediately apologizing for the insensitive comment
jokes.explanation: Leslie's immediate guilt over a mild joke shows her excessive politeness and consideration for others' feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, that was insensitive. I apologize.

jokes.text: Shoe shining has been a pretty wild ride, but is it possible there's something more out there for me?
jokes.setup: Andy reflecting on his career
jokes.punchline: Describes the most mundane job as an exciting adventure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy enthusiasm for the mundane. His ability to find excitement in shoe shining shows his positive outlook on everything.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know, I've had a lot of jobs in my life. I've been a shoe shiner, a shoe repair guy, a shoe... loser. But you know what? Every single one of those jobs was an incredible adventure. I mean, who else can say they've spent eight hours organizing alphabetically by heel height? That's not work, that's a calling.

jokes.text: One year from now, you, Andy Dwyer, will no longer be a shoe-shinist.
jokes.setup: April promising to help Andy find a new career
jokes.punchline: Creates the made-up word 'shoe-shinist' instead of 'shoe shiner'
jokes.explanation: April's deadpan delivery of a made-up professional title. The '-ist' suffix makes it sound more important than it is.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'll help you find a new career. You could be a shoe-shinist.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: A what?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: A shoe-shinist. Someone who shines shoes.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I think it's 'shoe shiner.'

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No, shoe-shinist. It sounds more professional.

jokes.text: Kyle, beat it. I'm going to make out with my wife now. But you've only done half of one of my shoes. Here. Now, I haven't done anything. Scram, kid.
jokes.setup: Andy wants to kiss April
jokes.punchline: Undoes his work to avoid responsibility while dismissing his customer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - removing the work means he never did anything wrong. Shows his childlike approach to responsibility.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: April, I really want to kiss you right now.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Then why don't you?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Because I'm at work and I have a customer.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey buddy, I'm gonna have to redo all of this work. Sorry about that.

jokes.text: Will you pledge right now not to raise taxes? I think that's premature. No pledge, no vote.
jokes.setup: Ron learning about Leslie's campaign
jokes.punchline: Immediately demands an anti-tax pledge
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron libertarian moment. His immediate jump to tax policy shows his consistent political philosophy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, I'm running for City Council!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You need to sign an anti-tax pledge. Immediately.

jokes.text: Leslie, I'm going to work my ass off for you. I'll do anything you ask me. I will prove myself. You don't even have to pay me.
jokes.setup: Andy being hired as Leslie's assistant
jokes.punchline: His enthusiasm leads him to volunteer to work for free
jokes.explanation: Andy's puppy-like enthusiasm leads him to devalue his own labor. Shows his genuine desire to help despite poor business sense.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'll do it for free!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No, Andy, you need to be paid.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? I get to work with you? I would pay you.

jokes.text: She has the tracking ability and body odor of a bloodhound.
jokes.setup: Ron explaining how Tammy One will find him
jokes.punchline: Compares his ex-wife to a hunting dog in unflattering terms
jokes.explanation: Brilliant insult that combines fear and disgust. The specific comparison to a bloodhound's tracking and smell is both precise and devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tammy One will find me. She's like a hunting dog, except instead of tracking birds, she tracks men who are trying to escape her.

jokes.text: That man has the largest penis I have ever seen. I actually don't even know if he has mumps. I forgot to look. I was distracted, by the largest penis I have ever seen.
jokes.setup: Ann reporting on her medical examination
jokes.punchline: Admits professional distraction led to incomplete diagnosis, then repeats the reason
jokes.explanation: Ann's professional composure completely breaks down. The repetition emphasizes her shock while admitting medical incompetence.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: The doctor said I'm perfectly healthy, but he was distracted during the exam because he kept staring at my... well, he didn't finish the full examination. But that's fine, I'm sure everything is fine.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Although, come to think of it, he did say he'd call me back because he forgot to check... actually, you know what, I bet he just wanted an excuse to call me.

jokes.text: It's not you, it's me. I'm not ready for a commitment. I just don't like you anymore. I know it's hard to hear that, but You're boring. And, frankly, you disgust me.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to make the breakup easier by being mean
jokes.punchline: Her attempts at cruelty become increasingly ridiculous and unconvincing
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of breakup clichés getting more absurd. Leslie's inability to be genuinely mean makes each insult more obviously false.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, I have to break up with you. And I'm going to be really mean about it to make it easier.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're... you're stupid!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, wait. You smell like a... like a foot.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A dirty foot.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's not even—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And your face looks like... like a regular face. But bad.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And I hope you step on a Lego. A really sharp one.

jokes.text: Leslie, there was a dude in the ladies' yacht club. Yeah, but I covered that pretty well.
jokes.setup: Ben explaining how he figured out the truth
jokes.punchline: Leslie thinks her obviously fake cover story was convincing
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Leslie's delusion about her deception skills. Her confidence in an obviously terrible lie is endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I knew something was off when you said you were at the dentist, but your teeth looked exactly the same.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I have naturally white teeth.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, you also said the dentist gave you a lollipop, but you had lipstick on your teeth.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It was a red lollipop!

jokes.text: Also, you've been making campaign speeches in your sleep. Granted, you always do that, but they got really specific and moving.
jokes.setup: Ben explaining how he figured out Leslie's campaign
jokes.punchline: Reveals Leslie always gives political speeches while sleeping, but these were different
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie character detail - sleep-talking politics is normal for her, but even her subconscious got more focused. Shows her total dedication to public service.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I realized these weren't Leslie's normal sleep speeches. Her normal ones are about zoning regulations and park maintenance. These were actual campaign speeches with policy positions and everything.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: So I knew something was up. Leslie was awake. She was actually running for office.

jokes.text: Or, perhaps, guilt for all the times I tried to smother you in your sleep.
jokes.setup: Tammy One claiming to want to help Ron
jokes.punchline: Casually admits to attempted murder during their marriage
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor delivered matter-of-factly. Establishes why Ron is terrified while making attempted murder sound like a minor marital quirk.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy One
dialogue.line: I'm here to help you, Ron.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Help me with what?

dialogue.speaker: Tammy One
dialogue.line: Well, back when we were married, I tried to kill you. Twice. But I'm over that now.

jokes.text: Sit up straight. You're not doing your breasts any favors. Thank you. Honey, your breasts look amazing.
jokes.setup: Tammy One criticizing Leslie's posture
jokes.punchline: Leslie thanks her for the insult, April immediately contradicts with support
jokes.explanation: Great contrast between Tammy One's casual cruelty, Leslie's politeness, and April's unexpected supportiveness. Shows all three personalities clearly.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy One
dialogue.line: Your posture is atrocious. You sit like a question mark.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Thank you so much for that feedback, Tammy.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Your posture is fine. You sit like an exclamation point!

tmdb_overview: Leslie is torn between Ben and her dreams of running for public office, while Ron braces himself for the arrival of his first wife, the original Tammy.
insight: Season premiere establishes Leslie's ambition with 61 jokes in 24 minutes—highest density opener of the run.
